concepts.name: Disruption Lens Applied Prospectively
concepts.definition: The electric-vehicle case uses the theory to analyze a contemporary technology before the outcome is known, showing disruption as a predictive diagnostic rather than only a historical explanation.

concepts.name: EV Foothold Markets
concepts.definition: Likely early markets are applications that value simplicity, short-range use, low emissions, or fleet routes more than highway range and refueling convenience, such as urban delivery or constrained local use.

concepts.name: Entrant Trajectory vs. Incumbent Metrics
concepts.definition: Electric vehicles may be inferior on range, speed, and refueling according to mainstream car metrics, yet improve along dimensions that could later matter widely.

arguments.claim: Good theory should help managers act before historical proof is obvious.
arguments.evidence: The EV chapter applies the same three-part test to a then-uncertain case rather than waiting for a complete retrospective dataset.

arguments.claim: EVs should not be forced first into mainstream highway commuting if their early strengths fit other jobs.
arguments.evidence: Disruptive strategy looks for customers who value the new attributes now, not mainstream customers who compare only with internal combustion vehicles.

cases.name: Electric Vehicles (1997 prediction)
cases.context: Written when EVs were nascent and pre-Tesla, with uncertain batteries, range, and market demand.
cases.illustrates: How to use foothold, initial inferiority, and improvement trajectory as a live diagnostic.
cases.lesson: Apply disruption theory prospectively to choose market entry, metrics, and organizational home.

logicChains.description: Define mainstream auto metrics → identify where EVs are inferior → locate customers/jobs that value EV attributes anyway → build in that foothold → improve batteries/cost/performance → reassess mainstream invasion potential.

terminology.definition: Using theory to reason about an emerging case before the market outcome is settled.
The mainstream performance frame that makes early EVs appear inferior on range, refueling, and speed.
An example early application where short routes and fleet economics can make EV attributes valuable.

nuance: The chapter is not a guarantee that any specific EV firm will win; it is a method for asking better strategic questions.
A disruptive technology can fail if managers choose the wrong first market or force it into mainstream metrics too early.
